title: Four-shaft overshot table mat
subtitle: A Goose-Eye overshot mat in wool and linen, finished 30 x 45 cm
excerpt: A traditional overshot table mat woven on a four-shaft floor loom in a Goose-Eye threading. Linen warp at 9 epi; wool pattern weft alternates with a linen tabby ground weft to build the overshot cloth. Finished mat measures approximately 30 x 45 cm after wet-finishing, which takes off around 10% in length and 5% in width.
sourceNotes: Overshot threading and treadling conventions from Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910), public domain, Internet Archive. Chapter IV covers two-tie-up structures and their traditional names. Mary Atwater pattern booklets (1920s-1930s) provide the canonical Goose-Eye threading sequence; publications from 1928 and earlier are in the US public domain. Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945), cited as standard reference for overshot sett and pattern-shot / tabby-shot alternation; written around, not quoted.

glossaryTerms.definition: A supplementary-weft weave structure in which pattern weft threads float over multiple warp threads to build a geometric figure. An overshot cloth uses two weft shuttles alternating: the pattern weft (heavier, coloured) makes the figure, and the tabby ground weft (finer, matches warp) locks each pattern pick in place.

In overshot weaving, the pick woven with the heavier pattern weft thread. Pattern shots create the visible geometric figure on the cloth face. Each pattern shot is followed by a tabby ground shot before the next pattern pick is thrown.

In overshot weaving, the plain-weave pick interlaced between pattern shots using the finer tabby weft. The tabby shot locks the pattern weft floats in position and keeps the cloth from loosening on the back. Without tabby shots, overshot floats slide out of position in washing.

The shortening of a woven cloth on the loom caused by the weft crimping over and under the warp threads. Take-up typically reduces the woven length by 5-8% compared to the measured warp length. It must be added to the warp calculation alongside shrinkage.

The additional warp and weft length calculated to compensate for the dimensional reduction that occurs when a woven cloth is wet-finished. Wool-and-linen overshot cloth typically shrinks 8-12% in length and 5-8% in width on its first wet-finishing.

content.text: Overshot is the classic colonial American and British weave structure for table mats, coverlets, and household textiles. This mat uses a Goose-Eye threading on four shafts: a linen warp sett at 9 epi (approximately 3.5 threads per cm), a coloured wool
pattern weft
, and a fine linen
tabby ground weft
alternating after each pattern pick. The finished mat measures approximately 30 x 45 cm after wet-finishing. On the loom, weave to 50 cm in length to allow for
take-up
of about 5% and
shrinkage
of approximately 10% on wet-finishing. The warp width should be set at 33 cm (allowing 3 cm draw-in across the width) to reach the 30 cm finished width.
The
overshot

structure produces a cloth with a geometric figure on one face and the inverse of that figure on the back. The Goose-Eye is a four-block pattern based on a rosette threading that creates a diamond-and-chevron figure across the mat width. It is one of the most widely documented traditional four-shaft overshot threadings in the British and North American guild canon.
What you need

items.name: Four-shaft floor loom with at least six treadles (eight preferred)
Weaving reed, 9-dent (or 8-dent at a slightly open sett)
Pattern weft: 2-ply wool in a dark colour (navy, forest, or burgundy)
Tabby weft: 16/2 linen, natural (same as warp)
Raddle (for spreading the warp when beaming)

content.text: The draft

The threading repeats a Goose-Eye block over 16 ends: shaft 1, 2, 1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 3, 4, 3, 2, 3, 2, 1, 2. Each full repeat produces one Goose-Eye unit across the cloth; two repeats across 32 ends fill the 33 cm warp width at 9 epi. The tie-up assigns treadles 1 through 4 to the four overshot pattern blocks (each treadle lifts two adjacent shafts) and treadles 5 and 6 to the two tabby sheds (treadle 5 lifts shafts 1 and 3; treadle 6 lifts shafts 2 and 4).

The treadling sequence alternates pattern treadles (1, 2, 3, 4, 3, 2, following the block sequence) with tabby treadle 6 after each pattern pick. The result is the Goose-Eye figure building up in the wool pattern weft, held flat by the linen tabby shots. To read the draft: the drawdown shows the cloth face as seen from the front; filled cells are where the weft lies on top of the warp.
Warp calculation
Work out the warp calculation in three parts: length, width, and total ends.

Length: the finished mat is 45 cm. Add 10% for shrinkage (4.5 cm) and 5% for take-up (2.25 cm) to get a weaving length of about 52 cm. Add 10 cm front loom waste (tie-on), 20 cm back loom waste and lease, and 10 cm for header and weft tails. Total warp length: approximately 92 cm. Wind to 100 cm.

Width: the finished mat is 30 cm. Add 5% for draw-in during weaving (1.5 cm) and 5% for width shrinkage on wet-finishing (1.5 cm). Working warp width: approximately 33 cm. At 9 epi that is 297 ends. Round down to 288 ends for an exact number of 16-end Goose-Eye repeats (288 / 16 = 18 repeats = 32.7 cm at 9 epi, which wet-finishes to approximately 30 cm).

Yarn quantity: 288 ends at 100 cm each equals 28,800 cm of warp. At the yardage of 16/2 wet-spun linen (approximately 5,000 m per 450 g skein), 29 m of warp yarn uses about 25-30 g. Plan for 50 g of linen warp to allow for the sampling and tying-on waste. The tabby weft uses roughly the same amount of linen as the warp length consumed (because a balanced sett means the weft length per pick equals the warp width). The wool pattern weft quantity depends on the beat density but plan for 80-100 g of 2-ply wool.
Warping and threading

Wind 288 ends (or the calculated count for your loom width) of 16/2 linen at even tension on the warping board, making the threading cross at the near peg. Secure the cross before removing the warp. Spread the warp through the raddle at the back of the loom, one end per slot, at the calculated thread count per centimetre. Beam onto the back beam under steady tension, winding sticks between each layer to separate the threads. Linen does not bloom like wool; it beams without packing sticks if the warp is tensioned evenly, but a thin stick between each layer prevents marking.

Wind 288 ends (or the calculated count for your loom width) of 16/2 linen at even tension on the warping board, making the threading cross at the near peg. Secure the cross before removing the warp. Spread the warp through the raddle at the back of the loom, one end per slot, at the calculated thread count per centimetre. Beam onto the back beam under steady tension, winding sticks between each layer to separate the threads. Linen does not bloom like wool; it beams without packing sticks if the warp is tensioned evenly, but a thin stick between each layer prevents marking.

Thread the heddles following the Goose-Eye draft: shaft 1, 2, 1, 2, 3, 2, 3, 4, 3, 4, 3, 2, 3, 2, 1, 2, repeating. Check the threading at each repeat boundary. A threading error in overshot becomes visible only when the pattern is woven; catching errors before sleying the reed saves taking the reed out later.

Sley the reed at the appropriate dent count for 9 epi. Tie on to the front apron rod in groups of 12-16 ends, equalising tension across the full warp width before tying the final bow knot. Check tension by pressing gently on each group; all groups should spring back with the same resistance.
Weaving the pattern

Tie up the treadles as shown in the draft. Wind the wool pattern weft onto the boat shuttle and the linen tabby weft onto the stick shuttle. Weave 10-15 picks of scrap linen as a header to spread the warp and establish the fell line.

Begin the overshot sequence. Press treadle 1 (pattern block 1), throw the wool pattern shuttle, and beat firmly. Before opening the next pattern shed, press treadle 6 (tabby shed 2), throw the linen tabby shuttle, and beat gently. Press treadle 2 (pattern block 2), throw the wool shuttle, beat firmly. Press treadle 6 again, throw the linen shuttle, beat gently. Continue: treadle 3, tabby 6; treadle 4, tabby 6; treadle 3, tabby 6; treadle 2, tabby 6; treadle 1, tabby 6. This is one full Goose-Eye figure sequence.

The beat differs between pattern and tabby picks. The wool pattern weft is heavier and benefits from a firm beat to pack it snugly. The linen tabby weft is finer and needs only a light beat; an over-beaten tabby pick pushes the pattern weft out of alignment and disrupts the figure. As a working check: the pattern weft should occupy roughly twice the vertical space of the tabby weft in the woven cloth. If the tabby and pattern weft rows appear the same height, the tabby is being beaten too hard.

Advance the warp every 8-10 cm. Check that the pattern blocks are forming consistently across the width; the Goose-Eye figure should be symmetrical about the centre of each repeat. If one side of the figure is compressed while the other is open, the treadling sequence has drifted from the prescribed order. Count the last four picks and confirm you are on the correct treadle before continuing.
Weave 10-15 picks of scrap yarn at the end of the mat before cutting off. This keeps the cloth from unravelling when the warp tails are handled.
Finishing

Cut the mat off the loom, leaving warp tails of at least 8-10 cm at each end. Secure the tails with overhand knots in pairs or groups of four ends close to the weaving. Weave in any loose weft tails using the blunt darning needle.

Wet-finish by submerging the mat in hand-warm water (around 30-35 C) with a small amount of gentle soap. Press gently and leave for 10-15 minutes. Press out the water between two towels without wringing. Lay flat on a clean surface, ease to the correct rectangular dimensions (30 x 45 cm), pin lightly if the cloth is pulling out of square, and leave to dry fully. Record the pre-wash and post-wash dimensions; linen-and-wool cloth typically takes off 8-12% in length and 5-8% in width on the first wash. Use this figure to calibrate the warp length if you weave additional mats from the same warp.

Trim the warp tail fringe or work a hemstitch before wet-finishing if a cleaner edge is preferred. Hemstitch is worked while the cloth is still on the loom: thread the blunt darning needle with warp yarn, and work a figure-of-eight stitch around groups of 4-6 warp ends, catching the last 2-3 picks of the weaving. Cut the cloth off after the hemstitch is complete.

A linen-and-wool overshot mat softens noticeably on its first wet-finish. The linen warp relaxes slightly as the sizing (applied during manufacture) washes out, and the wool pattern weft blooms into the spaces between the linen threads. The finished cloth has a hand distinctly different from the on-loom piece: firmer and more springy on the loom, softer and more drapey off it. This change is normal and expected; do not attempt to preserve the stiffness of the on-loom cloth by avoiding wet-finishing.

Store the finished mat flat or loosely rolled, not folded. A sharp fold crease in linen can become permanent if the cloth is stored under pressure. Wash subsequent times by hand in cool water; avoid hot water and agitation, which can felt the wool pattern weft over time.
Variations

The Goose-Eye threading supports several treadling variations beyond the standard sequence described above. Treadling blocks 1, 2, 3, 4 in a straight sequence builds the Goose-Eye figure. Reversing to 4, 3, 2, 1 mirrors the figure. Alternating blocks of 4 (1, 2, 3, 4, 4, 3, 2, 1) produces a diamond with a centre axis, sometimes called a rose-path figure.

The pattern weft and tabby weft colours can also vary. Weaving the entire mat with one pattern colour and one tabby weft colour produces a two-colour cloth. Changing the pattern weft colour partway through the mat (at a block boundary, not mid-repeat) creates colour stripes within the overshot figure without requiring a threading change.

A set of four mats from one long warp follows the same threading throughout. Each mat can carry a different pattern weft colour while sharing the same linen warp and tabby weft. Wind the warp to 400 cm (four mats at 100 cm loom length each) to allow for loom waste across all four.

items.problem: The Goose-Eye figure does not appear: the cloth shows irregular floats or a muddled pattern
items.cause: Threading errors in the heddles, or incorrect treadle tie-up
items.fix: Check the threading at each repeat boundary by pressing treadle 1 and looking at which ends rise. If the wrong ends rise, the heddle assignment is incorrect. Re-thread one repeat at the suspected error point and re-check before re-sleying the reed.

items.problem: The tabby picks are pushing up between the pattern picks, making the cloth thick and stiff
items.cause: The tabby weft is too heavy relative to the pattern weft, or is being beaten too hard
items.fix: Use a finer tabby weft than the pattern weft. If both are linen at 16/2, the tabby shuttle should carry a finer thread; 20/2 or 24/2 linen is more appropriate. Reduce the beat pressure on tabby picks.

items.problem: The selvedge wool pattern weft floats are loose and the edge of the mat looks uneven
items.cause: The first and last warp ends are not interlaced by the pattern treadles, leaving the pattern weft free at the selvedge
items.fix: Add one selvedge end on each side threading on shaft 1. These selvedge ends will be caught by the tabby picks even when the pattern treadle skips them. Alternatively, use a floating selvedge: a single end outside the main threading that the shuttle passes under on each pick regardless of shed.

items.problem: The finished mat is not rectangular after wet-finishing
items.cause: Uneven warp tension during weaving, or uneven beat
items.fix: Pin the damp mat to a flat surface in the correct rectangular dimensions immediately after pressing. Check with a set square at each corner. Leave pinned until completely dry. If the distortion is severe, re-wet the mat and re-pin.
